Day 31 - Learning ThinkPHP From Scratch: The End Is Just Another Beginning
I picked Day 31 as the wrap-up, so this post is mostly my closing thoughts on finishing the contest.
The end is just another beginning

This was my first time doing the Ironman contest, and honestly I spent the whole thing worried I wouldn't finish. I've got a memory like a goldfish, so I was terrified I'd slip up and forget to post in time and blow the streak. QQ
On top of that, I kept worrying my posts were low quality, or even

, or that I'd written something conceptually wrong. But then I thought about it: if I kept letting those fears stop me and ended up not entering at all, I'd regret it way more later. So I figured I'd just take the shot and see how it went.
series is done, but that doesn't mean the learning stops here. Information never runs out, after all. I've actually got another Ironman series still running too,
Re: Learning JS Life From Scratch
, so now I get to focus on just one topic.

When I decided to use this framework for the contest, a few people asked me, "Why not pick Laravel or Yii instead of something less common like ThinkPHP?" Personally, I don't think any framework or language is flat-out good or bad. What matters is whether it solves your problem. If it solves the problem, it's a good language and a good framework.

The other reason is that I'd used ThinkPHP before and found it pretty handy back then. It's a nice fit for beginners taking their first crack at a framework and building a feel for one, and the install isn't complicated either. The version I'd written back then was older though, ThinkPHP 3.2.4, which is why I challenged myself to write about ThinkPHP 5.1 for this contest.

When I was writing this series, ThinkPHP 6.0 had actually been out for around three months. But personally I feel like anything new needs about half a year before you can trust it to be stable, and ThinkPHP 6.0's architecture is different from ThinkPHP 5.1 on top of that. So I went with the more stable ThinkPHP 5.1.

I've written a few other series before this one. I started my tech blog (Is Ray Not Array) on 2019/04/11, and I've put out quite a lot in that time, covering things like Gulp, Express, Webpack, and Koa.js. When I first set up the blog the whole point was just to take notes for myself, but later some friends wanted me to teach them how to learn a few things, so I figured I'd just turn my notes into proper series and review the material for myself along the way. Here are those series in case they're useful to you. The wording and explanations might not be perfect, mind you, I'm still figuring things out myself (rookie smell all over me, XD).
This is about Gulp, not the drink, it's a task automation tool (article in Mandarin)
The Full-Stack Warrior's Road: Node.js (article in Mandarin)
→ builds a simple membership system together with Firebase
A little something about learning Webpack (article in Mandarin)
The legend of a full-stack warrior named Koa (article in Mandarin)
